text: The surgeon dissects straight down the midline towards the pubis and Cooper's ligament, which becomes visible. The dissection continues laterally in the true preperitoneal plane, keeping the transversalis fascia elevated, aiming to expose the chord structures.

The surgeon identifies a hernia medial to the epigastric vessels and proceeds to parietalize the cord structures. The obturator fat plug is visible, and the surgeon reduces it to ensure proper placement of the mesh's inferior edge.

Two 12x16 meshes are visible in the abdomen. The surgeon identifies a small lipoma and carefully reduces the fat laterally to ensure it extends beyond the inferior lateral edge of the mesh without amputating the fatty tissue.
The surgeon moves to the left-hand side to address the recurrent hernia, which is a direct defect. The hernia is visibly adhered to surrounding tissues, indicating it is quite stuck.

The surgeon carefully extracts the recurrent direct hernia sac from the defect, revealing sutures from a previous repair. The sac begins to come out more easily as the surgeon progresses, leaving some sutures in place while continuing the extraction.

The surgeon dissects and removes fat over the femoral area to check for any occult hernia, then performs similar dissection on the left side towards the obturator space. The obturator fat plug is reduced if the mesh will cover the area, and the cord on the left is parietalized.

The surgeon examines the left side for a lipoma, initially observing fatty tissue covering the canal but not extending into it. Posterior nodal tissue is identified covering the internal ring, and the surgeon avoids further dissection to prevent bleeding. The inferior edge of the mesh is positioned, and the hernia defects are assessed before placing the meshes overlapped at the midline.

The surgeon closes the flap starting at the midline, sewing towards the patient's left side. The lipoma is placed back inside the flap on the right side before closing the right side of the flap defect.
